title: Logistic Regression with R
text: Course Overview
crumbs: Course Overview

title: Logistic Regression with R
text:
Who this course is for
If you have completed the Linear Regression Short Course, you already have the main modelling idea: describe how an outcome changes with predictors, estimate the unknown parameters from data, and check whether the fitted model is useful. This course keeps that structure but changes the outcome model. Logistic regression is used when the outcome is binary, such as success/failure, yes/no, or pass/fail.
crumbs: Course Overview

title: Logistic Regression with R
text:
What you will learn

Understand how logistic regression extends the linear-model framework.
Recognize when logistic regression is appropriate for binary outcomes.
Translate between probability, odds, and log-odds.
Explain the Bernoulli/binomial error model for binary data.
Fit logistic models in R with glm() and read the output.
Interpret coefficients as odds ratios and communicate effects clearly.
Assess model performance with likelihood measures and classification metrics.
Make predictions and evaluate classification accuracy.
crumbs: Course Overview

title: Logistic Regression with R
text:
Prerequisites

You have completed the Linear Regression short course (or equivalent).
You can run basic R scripts and read simple model summaries.
crumbs: Course Overview

title: Logistic Regression with R
text:
Course map

1  Motivation: modeling binary outcomes motivates binary outcomes and introduces probability, odds, log-odds, and the logistic function.
From linear models to fitting logistic regression maps logistic regression onto the linear-model framework using GLMs, the logit link, and a Bernoulli/binomial error model, then Fitting logistic regression models fits logistic regression models with glm() and interprets coefficients, odds ratios, and confidence intervals.
Assessing logistic regression models assesses model fit with deviance, likelihood ratio tests, AIC, pseudo R-squared, and diagnostics.
Predictions from logistic regression models generates predicted probabilities, classifications, confusion matrices, ROC curves, and AUC.
Model building and a mini case study brings the workflow together in a mini case study with multivariable models, interactions, and reporting.
crumbs: Course Overview

title: Logistic Regression with R
text:
How to use this book

The course is designed to be completed sequentially: successive chapters build on prior concepts. However, it is possible to jump to specific sections as needed.
There is a glossary at the end for quick reference of key terms.
Each chapter includes exercises which should be attempted before continuing.
crumbs: Course Overview

title: 1  Motivation: modeling binary outcomes
text:
1.1 A quick review of linear regression
In the Linear Regression Short Course (LRSC), we used a linear model to predict an outcome variable \(Y\) from one or more predictor variables, \(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p\). Something like this:
\[
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + ... + \epsilon
\]
where \(\epsilon\) is an error term that captures the variability in \(Y\) that is not explained by the predictors. In the cases we considered, Y was a continuous variable (e.g., salary, Naplan score, etc.), and the model described how the expected value of the outcome, \(E[Y]\), changed as the predictors varied. For example, in the case of salary, the model described how the expected salary (\(E[Y]\)) of a worker changed as their years of experience (\(X\)) increased.
In many real-world situations, however, we want to predict an outcome that is not continuous. Instead, often what we want to predict or explain is a categorical variable. The simplest case of this is a binary outcome — that is, an outcome with only two possible categories (e.g. success/failure, yes/no, 0/1) 1.
crumbs: 1  Motivation: modeling binary outcomes

title: 1  Motivation: modeling binary outcomes
text:
Continue



Example 1.1: A binary outcome: pass or fail
Johno is a student studying for an important exam. He wants to understand how his study time affects his chances of passing the exam. To this end, he collects data on previous students’ study times and whether they passed or failed the exam. Here is a sample of the data Johno collected:

DataCode
















Johno wants to use this data to model the relationship between study time and the probability of passing the exam. He identifies his outcome variable, \(Y_i\), as the binary variable indicating whether student \(i\) passed or failed the exam, and his predictor variable, \(X_i\), as the amount of time that student spent studying. Therefore, Johno’s goal is to produce a statistical model that predicts \(Y_i\) (pass/fail) based on \(X_i\) (study time): \[
Y_i = f(X_i) + \varepsilon
\]



1.1.1 From categories to probabilities

1.1.1.1 The average of a binary outcome
Recall that the error term \(\epsilon\) in a regression model captures the difference between the observed outcome \(Y\) and the expected outcome \(E[Y]\) from the model.

Key-point: Expected value and error term
The expected value of a random variable \(Y\), denoted \(E[Y]\), can be thought of as the long-run average value of \(Y\) if we were to repeat the process that generates \(Y\) many times. That is, if we were to collect many samples of \(Y\) under the same conditions, the average of those samples would converge to \(E[Y]\). The error term \(\varepsilon\) represents the deviation of an observed value of \(Y\) from its expected value \(E[Y]\).
The error term \(\varepsilon\) is the difference between the observed value of \(Y\) and its expected value \(E[Y]\) i.e. \(\varepsilon = Y - E[Y]\). It captures the variability in \(Y\) that is not explained by the predictors in the model.
In regression models, we typically assume that the error term \(\varepsilon\) has a mean of zero (i.e., \(E[\varepsilon] = 0\)), which implies that the expected value of \(Y\) given the predictors is equal to the predicted value from the model (i.e., \(E[Y] = f(X)\)).


When \(Y\) was a continuous variable, \(E[Y]\) straightforwardly represented the average value of \(Y\) for given values of the predictors (e.g. the average (AKA expected, or predicted) salary of a worker of 9 years experience). But what is the ‘average’ of a binary (categorical) variable?

Continue

To model a binary outcome statistically, we have to represent it as a Random Variable rather than just a label. We can do this by coding the two categories as numbers, e.g. \(0\) and \(1\)2. For example, in Johno’s case

Example 1.2: Coding the binary outcome
We can code the outcome variable \(Y\) as follows: \[
Y =
\begin{cases}
\text{1} & \text{if student passes the exam} \\
\text{0} & \text{if student fails the exam}
\end{cases}
\]
in R,

DataCode



















Continue

The choice of using 0 and 1 here is not arbitrary. While we could have coded the categories as -1 and 1, or 0 and 2, the 0/1 coding has a special property that makes it particularly useful for modeling. In particular, when we code a binary outcome this way, the mean of the outcome variable, \(E[Y]\) has a direct interpretation: \[
E[Y] = P(Y=1)
\]
That is,

Key-point
The expected value (mean) of a binary variable coded as 0/1 is the probability of the outcome being 1. We label this probability as \(p\): \[
p = \Pr(Y=1) = E[Y]
\]


In Johno’s case, \(E[Y]\) represents the probability of passing the exam. ::: Example ### Mean of a binary variable as a probability If we ignore study time for a moment and just look at the overall pass/fail rates in Johno’s dataset, we can calculate the mean of the binary outcome variable: :::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output











1.1.2 Code
::::
This means that the probability of a randomly selected student in Johno’s dataset passing the exam is approximately webr round(mean(student_performance$pass_fail_num), 2).
:::

Continue



1.1.3 Why not use linear regression?
We now have a way to represent our binary outcome variable as a random variable, and we understand that the expected value of this variable has a meaningful interpretation as a probability.
A natural question at this point is: why not just use linear regression to model the relationship between \(X\) and \(E[Y]\)? After all, probabilities are continuous values between 0 and 1. We could fit a linear model like this: \[
p= E[Y] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X
\]

Example 1.3: When linear regression is not appropriate
Having completed the Linear Regression Short Course, Johno considers using linear regression to model the relationship between study time and the probability of passing the exam.
He first plots the data in a scatter plot with a fitted linear regression line:

PlotCode
















(note the use of geom_jitter() to add some random noise to the points so they don’t overlap exactly).
And obtains the following estimate for the effect of study time on the probability of passing: :::: {.panel-tabset} ### Output









1.1.4 Code
crumbs: 1  Motivation: modeling binary outcomes

title: 1  Motivation: modeling binary outcomes
text:
1.2 Log-odds (logit)
While odds are an improvement over raw probabilities, they are still bounded below by zero — which means a linear model for the odds could still, in principle, produce impossible negative predictions. We can fix this by taking one more step: the logarithm of the odds.

Key-term: Log-odds (logit)
The log-odds, or logit, of a probability \(p\) is defined as the natural log of the odds:
\[
\text{logit}(p) = \log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)
\]

PlotCode


















Because the logarithm stretches the odds scale — which runs from 0 to \(\infty\) — the log-odds scale runs from \(-\infty\) to \(+\infty\). This means we can safely model log-odds with a linear predictor without worrying about impossible predictions.

Key-point: The three scales



Scale
Range
Formula




Probability
\([0, 1]\)
\(p\)


Odds
\([0, \infty)\)
\(p / (1-p)\)


Log-odds
\((-\infty, +\infty)\)
\(\log(p / (1-p))\)



Moving from probability → odds → log-odds trades interpretability for a scale that plays nicely with a linear model.



Exercise 1.3: Computing log-odds
A student has a probability of passing of \(p = 0.75\). Using the logit formula, calculate their log-odds of passing.














p <- 0.75
log_odds <- log(p / (1 - p))
log_odds
p <- 0.75
log_odds <- log(p / (1 - p))
log_odds












1.2.1 The logistic function
If we model log-odds with a linear predictor, we need a way to convert predicted log-odds back into a probability. The function that does this is the logistic function (also called the inverse logit):
\[
p = \frac{e^{\eta}}{1 + e^{\eta}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\eta}}
\]
where \(\eta\) (eta) is the log-odds value. In R, this is plogis().

PlotCode
















The S-shaped curve of the logistic function captures exactly the kind of nonlinear relationship we expect between a predictor and a probability: as the log-odds increase, the probability rises steeply near 0.5 but flattens as it approaches 0 or 1.

Note: A useful symmetry
The logit and the logistic function are inverses of each other:
\[
\text{logit}(p) = \eta \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad p = \text{plogis}(\eta)
\]
You can think of the logit as the link from probability to the linear predictor, and the logistic function as the back-transform from the linear predictor to a probability.


This pair — a linear predictor on the log-odds scale, combined with the logistic function to convert back to probabilities — is the foundation of logistic regression, which we cover in the next chapter.
crumbs: 1  Motivation: modeling binary outcomes

title: 1  Motivation: modeling binary outcomes
text:
types of random variables are covered in more detail in the Inferential Statistics Short Course.↩︎
The use of 0/1 coding for binary outcomes is similar to the use of dummy variables for categorical predictors in linear regression, where each category is represented by a separate binary variable coded as 0 or 1.↩︎
Note that no student in the dataset has 0 study time, so this is an extrapolation.↩︎
crumbs: 1  Motivation: modeling binary outcomes

title: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression
text:
3 From linear models to fitting logistic regression
In Chapter 1 we introduced the practical problem: our outcome is binary, so the expected value of the outcome is a probability. We also saw the key mathematical move:
\[
\text{logit}(p) = \log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right),
\]
which lets us place probabilities on a scale that can be modelled with a straight line.
This chapter connects that idea to the Linear Regression Short Course. We will first replace the normal error model from linear regression with an error model that makes sense for binary data, then fit the resulting model with glm().
crumbs: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression

title: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression
text:
3.1 The linear regression template
In the Linear Regression Short Course, a simple linear model was written as
\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i,
\qquad \varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2).
\]
Equivalently, we can separate the model into two parts:
\[
Y_i \sim N(\mu_i, \sigma^2)
\]
and
\[
\mu_i = E[Y_i] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i.
\]
The first line describes how the observed outcomes vary around their mean. The second line describes how the mean changes with the predictor.

Key-point
Linear regression has both a mean model and an error model. The mean model is the straight line. The error model says observations vary around that line with normally distributed errors and constant variance.


In Chapter 1, we focused on the mean model for binary outcomes: how \(p_i = E[Y_i]\) might change with \(X_i\). We now add the missing error model.

Continue
crumbs: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression

title: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression
section: 3.2 The error model for a binary outcome
text:
3.2 The error model for a binary outcome
For Johno’s student example, each outcome is either 1 (pass) or 0 (fail):
\[
Y_i =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if student } i \text{ passes},\\
0 & \text{if student } i \text{ fails}.
\end{cases}
\]
If the probability that student \(i\) passes is \(p_i\), then the appropriate probability model is
\[
Y_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i).
\]
This is the one-trial version of the binomial distribution. It has two useful properties:
\[
E[Y_i] = p_i
\]
and
\[
\text{Var}(Y_i) = p_i(1-p_i).
\]
This variance is not constant. A probability near 0.5 gives more binary variation than a probability near 0 or 1. That is one reason the linear regression error model is not suitable here.

Key-point
For binary outcomes, the randomness is not represented by adding a normally distributed \(\varepsilon_i\) to a straight line. Instead, the observed 0/1 outcome is treated as a Bernoulli draw with event probability \(p_i\).



PlotCode














p <- seq(0.01, 0.99, by = 0.01)
plot(p, p * (1 - p), type = "l", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Probability of passing (p)",
     ylab = "Bernoulli variance: p(1 - p)")





Continue
crumbs: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression

title: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression
text:
3.3 Generalised linear models
A generalised linear model (GLM) has three pieces:

A distribution for the outcome.
A linear predictor.
A link function connecting the expected outcome to the linear predictor.

For linear regression, these pieces are:
\[
Y_i \sim N(\mu_i, \sigma^2),
\qquad
\mu_i = \eta_i,
\qquad
\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i.
\]
The link is the identity link because the mean itself is modelled directly by the linear predictor.
For logistic regression, the pieces are:
\[
Y_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i),
\qquad
\text{logit}(p_i) = \eta_i,
\qquad
\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i.
\]
The linear predictor is still linear. What changes is the outcome distribution and the link function.








Component
Linear regression
Logistic regression




Outcome
Continuous \(Y_i\)
Binary \(Y_i \in \{0,1\}\)


Distribution
Normal
Bernoulli / binomial


Mean
\(\mu_i = E[Y_i]\)
\(p_i = E[Y_i] = P(Y_i=1)\)


Link
Identity: \(\mu_i = \eta_i\)
Logit: \(\log(p_i/(1-p_i)) = \eta_i\)


Linear predictor
\(\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i\)
\(\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i\)


R function
lm()
glm(..., family = binomial)




Key-point
Logistic regression is not “nonlinear regression” in the usual sense. It is a linear model for the log-odds, paired with a Bernoulli/binomial error model and a logistic back-transformation to probabilities.



Continue
crumbs: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression

title: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression
text:
3.4 The logistic regression model
For the student example with study time as the predictor, the logistic regression model is
\[
Y_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i)
\]
and
\[
\text{logit}(p_i) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{studytime}_i.
\]
To return to the probability scale, we use the logistic function:
\[
p_i = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{studytime}_i)}}.
\]
In R, the inverse-logit transformation is plogis().

PlotCode














eta <- seq(-6, 6, length.out = 200)
plot(eta, plogis(eta), type = "l", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Linear predictor (eta)",
     ylab = "Probability")
abline(h = c(0, 1), col = "grey80", lty = 3)




The curve is S-shaped on the probability scale, but the model is still straight on the log-odds scale.

Continue
crumbs: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression

title: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression
text:
3.5 The formula in R
The R formula looks almost the same as the formula for lm():

OutputCode





Call:
glm(formula = pass_exam ~ studytime, family = binomial, data = students)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept)   0.3222     0.2818   1.143    0.253
studytime     0.1939     0.1311   1.479    0.139

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 500.50  on 394  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 498.27  on 393  degrees of freedom
AIC: 502.27

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4





fit_study <- glm(pass_exam ~ studytime,
                 data = students,
                 family = binomial)
summary(fit_study)




The formula pass_exam ~ studytime gives the linear predictor:
\[
\eta_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{studytime}_i.
\]
The argument family = binomial tells R to use the Bernoulli/binomial error model with the logit link. So the single glm() call contains the two changes we need for binary outcomes: a different error model and a different link.

Note
The default link for family = binomial is the logit link. Writing family = binomial(link = "logit") is equivalent, but more explicit.
crumbs: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression

title: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression
text:
3.6 Fitting logistic regression models
The coefficients \(\beta_0\) and \(\beta_1\) are the unknown parameters of the data-generating model. Fitting a logistic regression means estimating those parameters from the observed data.

3.6.1 From least squares to likelihood
For linear regression, lm() chooses the line that minimises the sum of squared residuals. This works because the model assumes normally distributed errors around a mean line.
For logistic regression, the outcome is 0 or 1, so squared residuals are no longer the natural fitting criterion. Instead, glm() uses maximum likelihood estimation. It chooses the coefficient estimates that make the observed sequence of passes and fails most likely under the Bernoulli model.

Key-point
Maximum likelihood asks: among all possible coefficient values, which values make the observed data most plausible under the model?


If student \(i\) has predicted probability \(p_i\), then a pass contributes probability \(p_i\) to the likelihood and a fail contributes probability \(1-p_i\). A good model gives high probabilities to the events that actually occurred.

Continue



3.6.2 Estimated coefficients
The estimated model is
\[
\widehat{\text{logit}(p_i)} = b_0 + b_1 \text{studytime}_i.
\]
As in the Linear Regression Short Course, we use Greek letters for unknown true parameters and Latin letters for estimates:



Symbol
Meaning




\(\beta_0\)
True intercept


\(\beta_1\)
True slope for study time


\(b_0\)
Estimated intercept


\(b_1\)
Estimated slope for study time




OutputCode




(Intercept)   studytime 
  0.3221862   0.1938849 





coef(fit_study)





Continue



3.6.3 Reading coefficients on the log-odds scale
The intercept is the estimated log-odds of passing when studytime = 0. In this dataset, no student has study time equal to 0, so the intercept is mainly needed to position the fitted curve.
The slope is the estimated change in log-odds for a one-unit increase in study time:
\[
b_1 = \widehat{\Delta \text{log-odds}}.
\]

Key-point
A positive coefficient means the predicted probability increases as the predictor increases. A negative coefficient means the predicted probability decreases. The coefficient itself is on the log-odds scale, not the probability scale.




3.6.4 Odds ratios
Exponentiating a coefficient converts it from a change in log-odds to an odds ratio:
\[
\text{odds ratio} = e^{b_1}.
\]

OutputCode




(Intercept)   studytime 
   1.380142    1.213957 





exp(coef(fit_study))




For studytime, the odds ratio compares two students who differ by one study-time category. If the odds ratio is greater than 1, the model estimates higher odds of passing for the student in the higher study-time category.

Exercise 2.1: Compute an odds ratio
Compute the odds ratio for studytime and store it as or_studytime.











or_studytime <- exp(coef(fit_study)["studytime"])
or_studytime
or_studytime <- exp(coef(fit_study)["studytime"])
or_studytime









Continue



3.6.5 Odds ratios are not probability differences
A fixed odds ratio does not correspond to a fixed difference in probability. The same multiplicative change in odds produces different probability changes depending on the starting probability.

OutputCode




  starting_probability probability_after_one_category probability_difference
1                  0.2                      0.2328283             0.03282828
2                  0.5                      0.5483200             0.04831995
3                  0.8                      0.8292299             0.02922990





or_studytime <- as.numeric(exp(coef(fit_study)["studytime"]))
starting_probability <- c(0.20, 0.50, 0.80)
starting_odds <- starting_probability / (1 - starting_probability)
new_odds <- starting_odds * or_studytime
new_probability <- new_odds / (1 + new_odds)

data.frame(
  starting_probability = starting_probability,
  probability_after_one_category = new_probability,
  probability_difference = new_probability - starting_probability
)





Note
Odds ratios can look large even when the absolute probability change is modest. For communication, pair odds ratios with predicted probabilities for realistic scenarios.
crumbs: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression

title: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression
text:
3.7 Inference for coefficients
The summary() output includes a standard error, a test statistic, and a p-value for each coefficient. These are Wald tests. They test whether a coefficient differs from zero on the log-odds scale.

OutputCode




             Estimate Std. Error  z value  Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept) 0.3221862  0.2818334 1.143179 0.2529641
studytime   0.1938849  0.1311284 1.478588 0.1392504





coef(summary(fit_study))




Wald confidence intervals can be computed with confint.default():

OutputCode




                  2.5 %    97.5 %
(Intercept) -0.23019720 0.8745696
studytime   -0.06312204 0.4508918





confint.default(fit_study)




It is often easier to report intervals on the odds-ratio scale:

OutputCode




                2.5 %   97.5 %
(Intercept) 0.7943769 2.397843
studytime   0.9388289 1.569711





exp(confint.default(fit_study))





Note
Confidence intervals for logistic regression coefficients are intervals for log-odds changes. Applying exp() converts them to odds-ratio intervals.
crumbs: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression

title: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression
text:
3.8 Adding predictors
The formula interface extends in the same way as multiple linear regression. A model with previous failures and absences is
\[
\text{logit}(p_i) =
\beta_0 +
\beta_1 \text{studytime}_i +
\beta_2 \text{failures}_i +
\beta_3 \text{absences}_i.
\]

OutputCode





Call:
glm(formula = pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + absences, family = binomial, 
    data = students)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)  1.08795    0.32965   3.300 0.000966 ***
studytime    0.03980    0.13931   0.286 0.775104    
failures    -0.96217    0.16995  -5.662  1.5e-08 ***
absences    -0.01843    0.01332  -1.384 0.166398    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 500.50  on 394  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 455.11  on 391  degrees of freedom
AIC: 463.11

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4





fit_multi <- glm(pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + absences,
                 data = students,
                 family = binomial)
summary(fit_multi)




Each coefficient is a partial effect on the log-odds scale, holding the other predictors in the model constant.
crumbs: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression

title: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression
text:
3.9 Categorical predictors
Categorical predictors work as they did in linear regression. R chooses a reference level and estimates comparisons against that level.

OutputCode





Call:
glm(formula = pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + schoolsup, family = binomial, 
    data = students)

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)   1.03285    0.31852   3.243  0.00118 ** 
studytime     0.06294    0.13961   0.451  0.65209    
failures     -0.98636    0.17230  -5.725 1.04e-08 ***
schoolsupyes -0.67531    0.32074  -2.105  0.03525 *  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 500.50  on 394  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 452.68  on 391  degrees of freedom
AIC: 460.68

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4





fit_support <- glm(pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + schoolsup,
                   data = students,
                   family = binomial)
summary(fit_support)




Here, schoolsupyes compares students with extra educational support to the reference group schoolsup = "no", after adjusting for study time and previous failures.
crumbs: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression

title: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression
text:
3.10 Chapter summary
Logistic regression follows the same modelling logic as linear regression:

Choose an outcome model.
Choose a linear predictor.
Estimate the unknown coefficients from data.
Use the fitted model for interpretation, prediction, and model checking.

The difference is that binary outcomes need a Bernoulli/binomial error model and a logit link. glm(..., family = binomial) fits this model by maximum likelihood. Coefficients are read on the log-odds scale, exponentiated coefficients are odds ratios, and predicted probabilities require the inverse-logit transformation. The next chapter asks whether the fitted model is adequate.
crumbs: 2  From Linear Models to Fitting Logistic Regression

title: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models
text:
4 Assessing logistic regression models
In linear regression, model assessment often starts with residual sums of squares and \(R^2\). Logistic regression uses a different error model, so the assessment tools also change. We still ask the same broad questions:
This chapter focuses on the first three questions. The next chapter focuses on prediction and classification performance.
crumbs: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models

title: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models
text:
4.1 Likelihood and deviance
Because logistic regression is fitted by maximum likelihood, model fit is naturally measured through the likelihood. R reports a related quantity called deviance.

OutputCode




         null_deviance     studytime_deviance multivariable_deviance 
              500.5040               498.2730               455.1066 





c(
  null_deviance = fit_null$deviance,
  studytime_deviance = fit_study$deviance,
  multivariable_deviance = fit_multi$deviance
)




Smaller deviance indicates a better fit to the observed data. The null model has only an intercept, so it represents a baseline model that predicts the same probability for every student.

Key-point
Deviance plays a role similar to residual sum of squares in linear regression: it is a lack-of-fit measure, so smaller values indicate better fit. It is likelihood-based rather than squared-error-based.



Continue
crumbs: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models

title: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models
text:
4.2 Comparing nested models
Two models are nested when one can be obtained from the other by removing predictors. The study-time model is nested inside the multivariable model because the multivariable model adds failures and absences.
We can compare nested logistic regression models with a likelihood ratio test:

OutputCode




Analysis of Deviance Table

Model 1: pass_exam ~ studytime
Model 2: pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + absences
  Resid. Df Resid. Dev Df Deviance  Pr(>Chi)    
1       393     498.27                          
2       391     455.11  2   43.166 4.232e-10 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1





anova(fit_study, fit_multi, test = "Chisq")




The test asks whether the added predictors improve the model enough to justify the extra complexity.

Note
Use likelihood ratio tests for nested models. For non-nested models, use information criteria such as AIC or compare prediction performance on a validation set.
crumbs: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models

title: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models
text:
4.3 AIC and model complexity
The Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) balances model fit and complexity. Lower AIC is preferred among the models being compared.

OutputCode




          df      AIC
fit_null   1 502.5040
fit_study  2 502.2730
fit_multi  4 463.1066





AIC(fit_null, fit_study, fit_multi)




AIC is useful when comparing several plausible models, but it is not a substitute for subject-matter judgement. A model with a slightly lower AIC but unclear interpretation may not be the best model for communication.

Continue
crumbs: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models

title: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models
text:
4.4 Pseudo R-squared
Linear regression’s \(R^2\) is based on sums of squares. Logistic regression does not have an exact equivalent. Several pseudo R-squared measures exist, but they should be treated as rough summaries rather than as direct analogues.
One common option is McFadden’s pseudo \(R^2\):
\[
R^2_{\text{McFadden}} = 1 - \frac{\log L_{\text{model}}}{\log L_{\text{null}}}.
\]

OutputCode




[1] 0.09070343





mcfadden_r2 <- 1 - as.numeric(logLik(fit_multi) / logLik(fit_null))
mcfadden_r2





Key-point
Do not interpret pseudo \(R^2\) as “the percentage of variance explained”. For logistic regression, likelihood-based comparisons and prediction checks are usually more informative.
crumbs: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models

title: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models
text:
4.5 Residuals and influence
Logistic regression residuals are less visually intuitive than linear regression residuals because the observed outcomes are only 0 or 1. Still, residual and influence diagnostics can help identify observations the model struggles to fit.

OutputCode




     fitted pearson_residual deviance_residual cooks_distance
1 0.7421124       -1.6963662        -1.6463484   0.0022419032
2 0.7491029       -1.7279171        -1.6629566   0.0023791303
3 0.1297399        2.5899286         2.0210016   0.0435756588
4 0.7632336        0.5569693         0.7351069   0.0004886744
5 0.7491029        0.5787315         0.7601039   0.0002668865
6 0.7277554        0.6116274         0.7972331   0.0003566034





diagnostics <- data.frame(
  fitted = fitted(fit_multi),
  pearson_residual = residuals(fit_multi, type = "pearson"),
  deviance_residual = residuals(fit_multi, type = "deviance"),
  cooks_distance = cooks.distance(fit_multi)
)
head(diagnostics)





PlotCode














plot(diagnostics$fitted, diagnostics$deviance_residual,
     xlab = "Fitted probability",
     ylab = "Deviance residual")
abline(h = 0, col = "grey70", lty = 2)




Large residuals mark cases where the observed outcome was surprising under the fitted model. Large Cook’s distances mark cases with unusual influence on the fitted coefficients.
crumbs: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models

title: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models
text:
4.6 Common problems

Assumption 3.1: Independent observations
The usual logistic regression model assumes observations are independent. If the same person, class, school, or site contributes multiple related observations, the model may need to account for clustering.



Assumption 3.2: Linearity on the log-odds scale
Continuous predictors are assumed to have approximately linear effects on the log-odds. This does not mean a straight line on the probability scale.


Other common problems include:

Separation: a predictor or combination of predictors perfectly separates passes from fails, leading to unstable or very large estimates.
Class imbalance: one outcome class is much more common than the other, so simple accuracy can be misleading.
Overfitting: too many predictors for the amount of data, especially when the event count is small.
crumbs: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models

title: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models
text:
4.7 Chapter summary
Assessing logistic regression requires likelihood-based thinking. Deviance, likelihood ratio tests, and AIC replace the least-squares tools used in linear regression. Residuals, influence checks, and assumptions still matter, but they must be interpreted through the binary-outcome model.
crumbs: 3  Assessing Logistic Regression Models

title: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models
text:
5 Predictions from logistic regression models
The fitted model gives a linear predictor on the log-odds scale:
\[
\hat{\eta}_i =
b_0 +
b_1 \text{studytime}_i +
b_2 \text{failures}_i +
b_3 \text{absences}_i.
\]
For most audiences, the useful output is not the log-odds. It is the predicted probability:
\[
\hat{p}_i = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\hat{\eta}_i}}.
\]
crumbs: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models

title: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models
text:
5.1 Predicting on different scales
By default, predict() returns values on the link scale:

OutputCode




       1        2        3        4 
1.054033 1.093833 1.133634 1.173434 





new_students <- data.frame(
  studytime = c(1, 2, 3, 4),
  failures = 0,
  absences = 4
)

predict(fit_multi, newdata = new_students)




For logistic regression, the link scale is the log-odds scale. To get predicted probabilities, use type = "response":

OutputCode




        1         2         3         4 
0.7415486 0.7491029 0.7565089 0.7637652 





predict(fit_multi, newdata = new_students, type = "response")





Key-point
Use type = "response" when you want predicted probabilities from a logistic regression model.



Continue
crumbs: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models

title: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models
text:
5.2 Scenario-based predictions
Predicted probabilities are often the clearest way to communicate a logistic regression model. We choose realistic predictor values and compare the model’s predictions.

OutputCode




  studytime failures absences predicted_probability
1         1        0        4             0.7415486
2         2        0        4             0.7491029
3         4        0        4             0.7637652





scenarios <- data.frame(
  studytime = c(1, 2, 4),
  failures = c(0, 0, 0),
  absences = c(4, 4, 4)
)

scenarios$predicted_probability <- predict(
  fit_multi,
  newdata = scenarios,
  type = "response"
)

scenarios




These are model-based probabilities, not guarantees. A student with a high predicted probability can still fail, and a student with a low predicted probability can still pass.

Exercise 4.1: Predict a probability
Compute the predicted probability for a student with studytime = 3, failures = 0, and absences = 2. Store it as p_case.











new_case <- data.frame(studytime = 3, failures = 0, absences = 2)
p_case <- predict(fit_multi, newdata = new_case, type = "response")
p_case
new_case <- data.frame(studytime = 3, failures = 0, absences = 2)
p_case <- predict(fit_multi, newdata = new_case, type = "response")
p_case









Continue
crumbs: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models

title: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models
text:
5.3 From probabilities to classifications
Sometimes we need to turn probabilities into predicted classes. To do this, we choose a classification threshold. With a threshold of 0.5, we predict a pass when \(\hat{p} \ge 0.5\).

OutputCode




         Actual
Predicted   0   1
        0  36  18
        1  94 247





threshold <- 0.5
class_hat <- ifelse(prob >= threshold, 1, 0)
conf_mat <- table(
  Predicted = factor(class_hat, levels = c(0, 1)),
  Actual = factor(students$pass_exam, levels = c(0, 1))
)
conf_mat




From the confusion matrix we can calculate performance measures:

OutputCode




   accuracy sensitivity specificity 
  0.7164557   0.9320755   0.2769231 





true_pos <- conf_mat["1", "1"]
true_neg <- conf_mat["0", "0"]
false_pos <- conf_mat["1", "0"]
false_neg <- conf_mat["0", "1"]

accuracy <- (true_pos + true_neg) / sum(conf_mat)
sensitivity <- true_pos / (true_pos + false_neg)
specificity <- true_neg / (true_neg + false_pos)

c(accuracy = accuracy,
  sensitivity = sensitivity,
  specificity = specificity)





Key-point
The threshold is a decision rule, not part of the fitted logistic regression model. Changing the threshold changes the classifications but not the predicted probabilities.
crumbs: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models

title: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models
text:
5.4 Threshold trade-offs
Lower thresholds predict more passes. Higher thresholds predict fewer passes. The right threshold depends on the cost of different mistakes.

OutputCode




  threshold  accuracy sensitivity specificity
1       0.3 0.7037975   0.9773585   0.1461538
2       0.5 0.7164557   0.9320755   0.2769231
3       0.7 0.7265823   0.8566038   0.4615385





thresholds <- c(0.3, 0.5, 0.7)

threshold_summary <- do.call(rbind, lapply(thresholds, function(t) {
  pred <- ifelse(prob >= t, 1, 0)
  cm <- table(
    Predicted = factor(pred, levels = c(0, 1)),
    Actual = factor(students$pass_exam, levels = c(0, 1))
  )
  tp <- cm["1", "1"]
  tn <- cm["0", "0"]
  fp <- cm["1", "0"]
  fn <- cm["0", "1"]
  data.frame(
    threshold = t,
    accuracy = (tp + tn) / sum(cm),
    sensitivity = tp / (tp + fn),
    specificity = tn / (tn + fp)
  )
}))

threshold_summary





Exercise 4.2: Accuracy at a different threshold
Using a threshold of 0.6, compute the classification accuracy and store it as accuracy_06.











threshold <- 0.6
class_hat <- ifelse(prob >= threshold, 1, 0)
accuracy_06 <- mean(class_hat == actual_num)
accuracy_06
threshold <- 0.6
class_hat <- ifelse(prob >= threshold, 1, 0)
accuracy_06 <- mean(class_hat == actual_num)
accuracy_06
crumbs: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models

title: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models
text:
5.5 ROC curves and AUC
An ROC curve shows the trade-off between sensitivity and false positive rate across many thresholds. AUC summarises that curve.

PlotCode











[1] 0.6483745





roc_thresholds <- seq(1, 0, by = -0.02)
actual <- students$pass_exam

tpr <- sapply(roc_thresholds, function(t) {
  pred <- ifelse(prob >= t, 1, 0)
  tp <- sum(pred == 1 & actual == 1)
  fn <- sum(pred == 0 & actual == 1)
  tp / (tp + fn)
})

fpr <- sapply(roc_thresholds, function(t) {
  pred <- ifelse(prob >= t, 1, 0)
  fp <- sum(pred == 1 & actual == 0)
  tn <- sum(pred == 0 & actual == 0)
  fp / (fp + tn)
})

roc_df <- data.frame(fpr = fpr, tpr = tpr)
roc_df <- roc_df[order(roc_df$fpr), ]

plot(roc_df$fpr, roc_df$tpr, type = "l", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "False positive rate",
     ylab = "True positive rate")
abline(0, 1, lty = 3, col = "grey60")

auc <- sum(diff(roc_df$fpr) *
             (head(roc_df$tpr, -1) + tail(roc_df$tpr, -1)) / 2)
auc





Key-point
AUC near 0.5 indicates little discrimination. Values closer to 1 indicate stronger discrimination between students who passed and students who failed.
crumbs: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models

title: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models
text:
5.6 Chapter summary
Prediction from logistic regression has two stages. First, use the fitted model to produce predicted probabilities. Second, if a classification is needed, choose a threshold that reflects the decision context. Probability predictions and classification decisions should be reported separately.
crumbs: 4  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models

title: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study
text:
6 Model building and a mini case study
The earlier chapters developed the pieces of logistic regression one at a time. This chapter brings them together into a short analysis workflow:
The goal is not to find the “best possible” student-performance model. The goal is to practise a defensible modelling workflow.
crumbs: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study

title: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study
text:
6.1 Define the outcome
We define the event as passing the final mathematics grade:
\[
Y_i =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } G3_i \ge 10,\\
0 & \text{if } G3_i < 10.
\end{cases}
\]

OutputCode





  0   1 
130 265 



        0         1 
0.3291139 0.6708861 





table(students$pass_exam)
prop.table(table(students$pass_exam))




Always report the event definition. Different cutoffs can answer different questions.
crumbs: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study

title: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study
text:
6.2 Choose predictors
A compact first model uses:

studytime: weekly study-time category.
failures: number of previous class failures.
absences: number of school absences.

These predictors are easy to explain and available before the final grade is known.

OutputCode





Call:
glm(formula = pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + absences, family = binomial, 
    data = students)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)  1.08795    0.32965   3.300 0.000966 ***
studytime    0.03980    0.13931   0.286 0.775104    
failures    -0.96217    0.16995  -5.662  1.5e-08 ***
absences    -0.01843    0.01332  -1.384 0.166398    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 500.50  on 394  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 455.11  on 391  degrees of freedom
AIC: 463.11

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4





fit_case <- glm(pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + absences,
                data = students,
                family = binomial)
summary(fit_case)




Odds ratios give a first interpretation:

OutputCode




             estimate     2.5 %    97.5 %
(Intercept) 2.9681706 1.5555872 5.6634800
studytime   1.0406030 0.7919686 1.3672947
failures    0.3820611 0.2738250 0.5330802
absences    0.9817405 0.9564488 1.0077010





exp(cbind(
  estimate = coef(fit_case),
  confint.default(fit_case)
))





Key-point
Use odds ratios to summarise model coefficients, but use predicted probabilities to communicate the practical size of effects.



Continue
crumbs: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study

title: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study
section: 6.3 Compare a small set of models
text:
6.3 Compare a small set of models
We compare three models:

OutputCode




          df      AIC
fit_null   1 502.5040
fit_study  2 502.2730
fit_case   4 463.1066


Analysis of Deviance Table

Model 1: pass_exam ~ studytime
Model 2: pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + absences
  Resid. Df Resid. Dev Df Deviance  Pr(>Chi)    
1       393     498.27                          
2       391     455.11  2   43.166 4.232e-10 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1





fit_null <- glm(pass_exam ~ 1, data = students, family = binomial)
fit_study <- glm(pass_exam ~ studytime, data = students, family = binomial)
fit_case <- glm(pass_exam ~ studytime + failures + absences,
                data = students,
                family = binomial)

AIC(fit_null, fit_study, fit_case)
anova(fit_study, fit_case, test = "Chisq")




This mirrors the model-building logic from the Linear Regression Short Course: start simple, add predictors for clear reasons, and check whether the added complexity is useful.
crumbs: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study

title: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study
text:
6.4 Consider interactions carefully
An interaction allows the effect of one predictor to depend on another. For example, the association between absences and passing might differ depending on whether a student has previous failures.

OutputCode




                df      AIC
fit_case         4 463.1066
fit_interaction  5 463.9819



Call:
glm(formula = pass_exam ~ studytime + failures * high_absence, 
    family = binomial, data = students)

Coefficients:
                         Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   
(Intercept)               0.79818    0.34899   2.287   0.0222 * 
studytime                 0.04476    0.13851   0.323   0.7466   
failures                 -0.67971    0.23208  -2.929   0.0034 **
high_absencelow           0.27664    0.25978   1.065   0.2869   
failures:high_absencelow -0.59156    0.35360  -1.673   0.0943 . 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 500.50  on 394  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 453.98  on 390  degrees of freedom
AIC: 463.98

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4





fit_interaction <- glm(pass_exam ~ studytime + failures * high_absence,
                       data = students,
                       family = binomial)

AIC(fit_case, fit_interaction)
summary(fit_interaction)




Interactions can be useful, but they make interpretation harder and require enough data in each subgroup.

Note
Add interaction terms when they answer a real question or are strongly motivated by the context. Do not add them only to search for significance.
crumbs: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study

title: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study
text:
6.5 Communicate with scenarios
Scenario predictions translate the model back to the probability scale.

OutputCode




  studytime failures absences predicted_probability
1         1        0        2             0.7485492
2         2        0        2             0.7559662
3         4        0        2             0.7703504





scenarios <- data.frame(
  studytime = c(1, 2, 4),
  failures = c(0, 0, 0),
  absences = c(2, 2, 2)
)

scenarios$predicted_probability <- predict(
  fit_case,
  newdata = scenarios,
  type = "response"
)

scenarios




For a non-technical audience, this table is usually more useful than a coefficient table.
crumbs: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study

title: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study
text:
6.6 Summarise predictive performance

OutputCode




         Actual
Predicted   0   1
        0  36  18
        1  94 247


   accuracy sensitivity specificity 
  0.7164557   0.9320755   0.2769231 





prob <- predict(fit_case, type = "response")
threshold <- 0.5
pred <- ifelse(prob >= threshold, 1, 0)
cm <- table(
  Predicted = factor(pred, levels = c(0, 1)),
  Actual = factor(students$pass_exam, levels = c(0, 1))
)
cm

accuracy <- sum(diag(cm)) / sum(cm)
sensitivity <- cm["1", "1"] / sum(cm[, "1"])
specificity <- cm["0", "0"] / sum(cm[, "0"])

c(accuracy = accuracy,
  sensitivity = sensitivity,
  specificity = specificity)




This is an in-sample summary, so it is optimistic. In a real predictive project, we would use a validation set or cross-validation.
crumbs: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study

title: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study
text:
6.7 Reporting template
Use this structure for a short logistic regression report:

Outcome: Define the event and report the event rate.
Model: State the predictors and the link function.
Effects: Report key odds ratios with confidence intervals.
Predictions: Give predicted probabilities for realistic scenarios.
Performance: Report threshold, confusion matrix metrics, and any AUC or validation results.
Limitations: Note missing predictors, observational design, sample size, class imbalance, and any modelling assumptions.

Template paragraph:

We modelled the probability of passing the final mathematics assessment using logistic regression. The event was defined as G3 >= 10. The model included study time, previous failures, and absences. Coefficients were estimated on the log-odds scale and reported as odds ratios. To make the results easier to interpret, we also calculated predicted probabilities for representative student profiles. Using a threshold of 0.5, we summarised classification performance with accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. These performance results are in-sample and should be validated before using the model for decision making.
crumbs: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study

title: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study
text:
6.8 Chapter summary
Logistic regression analysis follows the same disciplined pattern as linear regression: define the response, build interpretable models, compare alternatives, check assumptions and performance, and communicate effects on a scale the audience can understand. The main difference is the scale: coefficients live on the log-odds scale, while conclusions are usually clearest as predicted probabilities.
crumbs: 5  Model Building and a Mini Case Study

title: Glossary
text:
Accuracy
The proportion of all predictions that are correct.


Akaike Information Criterion
A model comparison metric that balances fit and complexity; lower values indicate a preferred model among those compared.


AUC
Area under the ROC curve; a summary of classification performance across thresholds.


Binary outcome
A response variable with two possible values, often coded as 0 and 1.


Binomial distribution
A probability distribution for the number of events in repeated binary trials, with event probability p.


Calibration
How closely predicted probabilities match observed event rates.


Class imbalance
When one outcome class is much more frequent than the other.


Classification threshold
The cutoff used to convert predicted probabilities into classes (for example, predict 1 if p >= 0.5).


Coefficient
A model parameter that describes how a predictor changes the log-odds, holding other predictors constant.


Confidence interval
A range of plausible values for a model parameter based on the data.


Confusion matrix
A table of predicted versus actual outcomes used to summarize classification performance.


Deviance
A goodness-of-fit measure based on the likelihood; lower is better.


Factor
A categorical predictor in R that is represented by discrete levels.


False negative
A case where the true class is 1 but the model predicts 0.


False positive
A case where the true class is 0 but the model predicts 1.


Generalized linear model
A model class that combines a response distribution and a link function to relate predictors to the expected response.


Intercept
The expected log-odds when all predictors are set to zero (or to their reference values).


Interaction
A term that allows the effect of one predictor to depend on another.


Likelihood
A measure of how well a model explains the observed data; higher is better.


Likelihood ratio test
A test that compares nested models using differences in deviance.


Linear predictor
The linear combination of predictors, for example beta0 + beta1 x.


Link function
A function that connects the mean of the response to the linear predictor; for logistic regression the link is the logit.


Logit
The log of the odds: log(p / (1 - p)).


Logistic function
The S-shaped function that maps any real-valued input to a probability between 0 and 1.


Logistic regression
A regression model for binary outcomes that models the log-odds of an event as a linear function of predictors.


Maximum likelihood estimation
A method for estimating model parameters by choosing values that maximize the likelihood of the observed data.


Multivariable logistic regression
A logistic regression model with multiple predictors included simultaneously.


Nested models
Two models where one is a special case of the other (typically by removing one or more terms).


Null model
A model with no predictors, containing only an intercept.


Odds
The ratio of the probability of an event to the probability of it not occurring: p / (1 - p).


Odds ratio
The multiplicative change in odds for a one-unit increase in a predictor; exp(coefficient).


p-value
The probability, under a null hypothesis, of observing a result at least as extreme as the one obtained.


Predicted probability
The model’s estimate of the probability of an event for a given set of predictors.


Probability
A number between 0 and 1 that represents the chance of an event.


Pseudo R-squared
A likelihood-based summary of model fit for logistic regression that is used for rough comparison, not as a direct analogue of linear-model R-squared.


Random variable
A numerical variable whose value depends on the outcome of a random process.


Reference level
The baseline category for a factor against which other levels are compared.


ROC curve
A curve showing the trade-off between sensitivity and 1 - specificity across thresholds.


Saturated model
A model that fits the observed data perfectly by using as many parameters as needed.


Sensitivity
The proportion of true positives correctly identified by the model.


Separation
A situation where predictors perfectly separate outcomes, leading to unstable estimates.


Specificity
The proportion of true negatives correctly identified by the model.


True negative
A case where the true class is 0 and the model predicts 0.


True positive
A case where the true class is 1 and the model predicts 1.


Wald test
A hypothesis test that uses an estimated coefficient and its standard error to assess evidence against a null value.
crumbs: Glossary
